IRISH BOOK LOVER

DEOEMBER, 1910. No. 5

THE ACHILL PRESS.
By Rev. R. S. MAFrrETT.

Under ‘“ Achill ”’ Archdeacon Cotton says:—*‘‘ The Rev. Edward
Nangle introduced a printing press into the island, at the Mission
House at Dugarth, and established a religious periodical, entitled ‘The
Achill Missionary Herald ’; the first number of which appeared on
July 3lst, 1837, printed at the Mission Press, by Willlam Pugh.”” As
far as I can ascertain we are practically dependent upon the above
‘““Herald "’ for facts with respect to the Achill press. I have found

no reference to it in Madden or Lowndes, nor further information from

Power or in the ‘¢ Life of the Rev. E. Nangle,”” who, we are told, first
visited the island in 1831, in a' time of famine, and, after the “ settle-
ment "’ was established in 1833, took up his residence as clergyman in
1834. Trom the ‘“ Herald »’ for April, 1865, we learn that the printing
Press, a gift of ‘‘ some friends in London and York,” arrived at the
Settloment in December, 1835. Mr. Nangle, in an extract from his
lournal, tells us that owing to the high winds the press could not be
landed for some days from the hooker, which had to be moored in the
bay and left by the men on board to the mercy of the wind and waves.
The question at once suggests itself whether there was no output from
the press during the year and a half which elapsed before the pubhca-
z.t'”ﬂ of the first number of the ° Herald.” This first number, as I
have learned from a member of his family, was «“ set up by the Re‘v
Nangle himself. e
 Of the printing of tlus pross I have in all fourteen 1tems, mth re-
3Dﬁct % six of which I am not aware as yet of any additional reference -

“« THREE IRISHMEN I EKNEW.”

When in the sixties, my brother Edmund was a student at law of
the Middle Temple, through him I became acquainted with a number
of Templars. Among these were three Irishmen, N. ¥. Davin, J. L.
Molloy, and D. L. Macafee. Davin was a tall spare man with a vy
thin purse, and he had to live as best he could by means of journalism
I have a note that his name is in Appleton’s American Cyclopedi
1889, vol. xii, p. 667, and that he was born at Kilfnane, Treland, 13th
January, 1843. He was called to the English Bar in 1868, and I was
one of the guests at his call party. He never obtained any Pmctice,
his time heing all taken up with journalistic work to get a living. He

pher. He took a great interest in my *‘ Handbook of Tictitiot®
Names,”” and gave me numerous notes for tlrat book. Among theso W&
his full Christian name as ‘‘ Nicholas I'rancis I'lood Davin,”’ though "_0'
cording to Foster’s ¢ Men at the Bar,’ ’ he entered himself in the book?
of the Middle Temple without the * Francis.” He was editor of the

.« gtandard ”’ and the ‘‘ Irish Times.”” After 1872 I lost sight of him
and never heard of him again, until one day about three years ago I

‘was staylng at a hotel at Brighton, and I took up a book that be-
]bnged to another visitor entitled, ‘‘ From the great lakes to the wide

west,”” by Bernard McEvoy, published in 1902, on p. 274 I came across

the following :—
« T must here put down some record of an interview I had at Re-

Igina with one who was from many points of view the most remarkable
man there, and whose recent tragic death gives a melancholy interest
to the circumstances as I look back upon them. I had been to the Post
Office for my mail on the morning after my arrival (at Winnipeg om
91st October, 1901) and was walking towards the main street cf the
town, when I overtook a tall, well set-up figure in a black velvet coat,
and wearing a white soft hat. I soon saw that it was Mr. Davin, and
introduced myself, calling to mind an Irish journalist’s dinner we both
attended years before. He was delighted to meet me again, and I went :
with him to his rooms, which were situated over the office of ‘‘ The
Leader” newspaper with which he was formerly connected. There was
a sign on the door ‘‘ N .F. Davin, Advocate,” and we went upstairs
to find a spacious room very light and pleasant, and with windows that
looked out over the somewhat scattered houses of Regina to the distant
prairie. FESL, '
““ He looked very, well, and seemed full of spirit and energy. There
was a wonderful simplicity and charm in his manner.” Davin gave Mec-
Evoy a copy of his speech on the first parade of the Strathcona Horse,
Tth March, 1900. McEvoy refers to Davin’s death on p. 277, and his
name is indexed as ¢ the late,’ and he is referred to as a well-known
man, He died under painful circumstances having shot himself on
Friday, 18th October, 1901. | .
McEvoy’s description tallied so accurately with my recollections of
Davin that T folt convinced it was my old friend. But I was doubtful,
ﬂ}“m are so many people of the same name and I had never heard of
hig lﬁaving England. I then showed the "pa.‘ssaiges to the gentleman
from Calgary, to whom the book belonged who was on a visit to'
England. Without .my saying anything about the initials h,_ez.f-'tit;.: once
8:?0 the full christion names. ‘ Yes,” he said, I knew Nicholas: e
'Jﬁd Davin, he was senator for some time.”” His lalfgﬁst-:‘_’ﬁifﬁ‘l‘-‘k 13 SR

GOSSIP.

The ** Books Wanted ”’ section of the ‘¢ Publisher’s Circular,” is a
unwitting source of mirth to many. Here is the latest *‘ Howler) |

«« Stracham’s Selections from old Irish Horses!”
Some time ago I referred to the unique collection of books and
ERER pamphlets formed by Thomas Davis, and now in the possession of Mr.
i Westall, 106, Charing, Cross Road. Mr. Westall informs me that he
LEE has now arranged them, and offers the lot to any intending purchaser
for £30. The collection certainly should find a homa in some Irish pub-
lic library, at this reasonable price. | |
Although the * Wm. Harrison Ainsworth and his Friends,” by &
M. Ellis (John Lane, 32s. net.), one of the finest literary hiographies
published for many years, does not come within our purview, yet rish
readers will find much of interest in it, pertaining to those great Core
gians—Maclise, Maginn, Kenealy, and Father Prout, and their sayIng
and doings in the great Metropolis, not to mention excellent illust
tions of the houses wherein these lines are written and printed. !

The ‘¢ Saturday Review "’ asks:i—

Does an [rish-American really speak the language of‘Mr. Dooley 7
1f he does talk the phonetics of Mr. Dooley’s l)fjok, he has 01'11-, Sym-
hy. Certainly he should lose no time in seve:rlng. the home ties that
remain o him. As for the true Irish accent, he 1's a bold man. t}'I&t
¢rios to get it on to paper. Syngoe did not attempt it, and Mr. Kipling

od better have left it alone. We cannot believe that Mr. Dooley’s

pat

h
mechanically perverse orthography represents any language or accent

under the sun. We know it is not Irish.”
REVIEWS.

Ax Ounrve Brancm 1N IRELAND. (Macmillan.) Mr. Willilam
O’'Brien here gives the full history of the rise and progress of t:ﬁg Al-l—
tor-Ireland movement, and of the Conciliation policy with 'W]J.ICh hl_s
public life has been idontified for some years. He traces, in graphic
language, the whole course of Irish politics from the time of the Parnell
disaster, and points out that the English public does not seem to ]{ave
realised that ‘¢ the new method > of dealing with the Irish question,
discovered in 1902 has brought about a beneficent revolution in the
country. The book reveals for the first time the inner workings of the
futile ;Boulogne Conference, to which the writer made his way with s.o
much difficulty, and left disheartened to enter a prison cell. It 1s
written in a ’Lllgfﬂllghly readable manner, Mr. O’Brien is master of a
forcilile English style; and oxcellent portraits are given of. eightﬂ pPromi-
nent men who are associated with the movement. o ‘

Mr. O’Brien tells us inter alia that he made £1,800 by the, Pubhca-'-
tion of his first novel, ¢ When we were Boys,”’ written in Galway Gaol.

whilst every page bears evidence of the remarkable knowledgg of s
author and his wide linguistic attainments. The only blot is the atte
to reproduce the cockney accent of the old soldier , Which is unnecggml:,t
and had better been left unattempted. We are Promige( .
complement of this romance, which we close, in the firm belief, that ,
very great novelist has passed away in the person of Robert Elliot,

The current part of * The English Historical Review ’ (Long.
man’s), contains several letters discovered by Dr. Holland Rose amongst
the Pitt Papers, written by spies at Hamburg and Brussels i 1798,
Dr. Rose suggests that it was these that gave the information to
ministry that led to the arrest of Father 0’Coigly and Arthur Q’ Conngr
at Margate, and not as W. J. Fitzpatrick concluded in his ““ Secret
Service under Pitt,’’ that supplied by Samuel Turner. It also gives (fron
the Chatham MSS., 315), ‘‘ Minutes of a conversation between MM,
Marsden and Dr. Mcl\evm, Arthur O’Connor and J. (sic.) Addis Fm.
met, State Prisoners.”” In a foot-note Dr. Rose makes the sad mistake
of stating that Addis Emmet was the ‘‘ father of Robert!”’

WINDELE’S CoRrk *(Guy and Co., Cork).—Our esteemed contributor,
Mr. James Coleman, M.R.S.A.I., has edited with care, skill, and taste
a new edition of this valuable httle work, which has been out of print
for many years. The valuable additions, notes, and a,ppendm bringing 1t
down to date, the results of wide reading and research, add much to the
value of the original work and the illustrations of spots long swept
away in the march of improvement will be highly appreciated by the
scattered sons of the city on the Lee.

As becomes the premier county, TiPPERARY’s ANNuaL (Long and
O’Connor, Clonmel), is first in the field. The success of last year’s 1881
has »emboldened the promoters to enlarge the present. volumag, whicll
is turned out in capital style and is readable from cover to cover. Bak
lads, poems, hiographies, tales and topographical sketches appeal %0 all

tastes and the illustrations are well reproduced and vaaluu.ble-—eﬂpecr“  (

ally that of Smith O’Brien and Meagher in Clonmel Gaol. The wri g
of the interesting sketch of John Sadleir may care to know that 111‘
career of crime was utilised by Dickens as the ‘* wonderful Merdle,” in
“* Littie Dorrrb ” by Lever in * Davenport Dunn,” :md by J fo}.’h

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 73
FORTHCOMING WORKS.

Seosamh Macathmoil’s new book of impressions of Donegal, *“ Sea
Ribbons and Ling,” is to be issued by Messrs. Maunsell in Novem-
ber. This, the first prose volume of a true poet will make interesting
reading 1f we may judge by a portion of the MS. submitted to us.

The Memoirs of that distinguished Irish soldier and scholar, Sir
william Butler, K.C.B., which he had completed before his death, are
now in the press. That they will be highly entertaining goes without
saying, and we learn from a reliable source that they will contain some
sensational revelations.

1)

Mr. Alfred P. Graves, who has recently retired under the ‘‘ sixty-
" rule, certainly shows no evidence of diminished literary capacity,
if one can judge by recent issues of ‘‘ The Times,”” ‘‘ The Mail,”” and
“T.P.’s Weekly.”” He is editing for Messrs. Dent, ‘‘ The Shakespeare
Circle,”” which he appropriately dedicates to his old friend, Prof.
Dowden, the eminent Shakespearean authority. The volumes are
primarily intended for Shakespeare Reading Societies, and will be en-

five

tiched with notes derived from the leading authorities on the poet, but

we feel they will appeal to a still wider circle of readers at home and
abroad.

papers there referred to as in the Magee College Library we are indeb
ted to the great kindness of Sir Edward Reid. He purchased them, |
helieve, at the auction of the valuable library of the late Pitt Skipton,
Esq., Beechhill, and, when leaving this city to reside in London, he }
presented them to our library, that what he believed to be ‘ umque
memorials of the older history of the city > might be 1n safe keeping for
it. They comprise the issues of the ‘ Derry Journal’ for the years
1772 (from the first number), 1773, 1791, 1793, 1794, and 1797 to 18%. |
It is believed that there is no other extant copy of the 1772-73 wlume-‘. ::'_!.‘

TaE OrANGE Sociery (Vol. ii., pp. 47, 60.)—Mr. Frankfort Moore,
the popular novelist, in his ¢ Journalist’s Note Book,”’ gives a most
thumorous description of his chief, Richard Lilburn, writing his * His.
tory of the Orange.”” The poor old man became obsessed with his sub
Ject, and almost buried beneath his mass of material. None the less, the
work 1s well worth reprinting. J. H.§

-overlooked. It was published some ten years ago, and is still in print.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

The inaugur 1l lecture of the winter session was delivered by Miss
Tithel Rolb Wheeler, on the 5th November, with Mr. Herbert Tr ench In

the chair.

The subject chosen was ‘‘ Irish Ideals in Poetry and Drama,”’ and *-?j
\iss Wheeler llustrated her delightful lecture with quotations from { 1
Mangan, ¢ A. E.,”” W. B. Yeats, Synge, Padriac Colum, Lady Gregory, Y

na Carbery, Herbert Trench, A. P. Graves, G. B. Shaw, Arthur ~

Etl
0'Shaughnessy, Miss Eva Gore-Booth, Stephen Gwynn, Miss Alice Mil- - A

ligan, Joseph Campbell, Miss L. Grifin, Edward Martyn, Douglas {
Hyde, S. L. Robinson, and Miss Emily Lawless. In her own words she 11 8§84

« goted as show woman,’”’ and allowed the pr esent-day writers on Ire-

land to reveal their ideals.
She divided Ideals first under three heads: The Indefinite Ideal,

those vague longings, those aspirations constantly frustrated, that are
at once the spur and the anguish of the Irish people. In connection
with this Miss Wheeler touched upon the attitude of the Irish towards
Nature, and their love of Music, as well as their quality of dreaming.
The Mystical Ideal at which those poets aim who believe the trance-
condition is a necessary preliminary to revelation. And it is their gift
that they are able to create an enchanted atmosphere, in which we
know ourselves in the presence of old unquiet mysteries and strange | Sy
spirits. The National Ideal. This third section, the lecturer sub-divi- g -
ded into Ireland considered mythically, historically and ideally, and | i1
Ireland considered practically. In mythical Ireland we get the musm {f e ';
of splendour and of triumph, but in historic Treland the low dirge of : " e
the funeral march and wild keening over the dead. While in ideal . T
Ireland we get ‘‘ Dark Rosaleen,’”’ the pa.ssmna.tely loved sweetheart g ok
ad queen. .Among the practical ideals that Irish writers have en-
Shrined in literature are the Ga@hc League, Co-operative farming, a,nd
.thﬁ National Theatre. In conclusmn, the lect.urer said ¢ Two conﬂm’o—
Say st How to feed the body and keep. inviolate the soul '«Eh‘._ TS
Problem ig an old one, but 1t 18 s*bﬂl one of e\treme comple‘uty W & - l

{"“’mt the genius who will reconclle! these two ideals.” S

““Tea-Table Talk’ on ‘‘The Ulster Origin of E. A. Poe and the Bronteg »
Founding his remarks upon the published works of Sir Edmung Bewl B
in the one instance, and those of Dr. Wright, Ramsden and Hgrshu
Turner in the other, he clearly traced the origin of the Americap Post
to David Poe of Cavan, and that of the famous novelists to the Brunty,

dT-F-_.‘

-
= -

of Co. Down. But in addition, and this rendered the ‘‘talk’ much More
attractive, Mr. Vesey Knox related some conversations with old ““ ro.
sidenters '’ in the district, contemporaries of Hugh and Welch, the
uncles of the immortal sisters, and detailed some anecdotes regarding
them, obtained from a Mr. Frazer who knew them all, which have neyep
been published. Mr. Vesey Knox was ‘‘ born and bred '’ in Co. Dowr, |
some fifteen miles from the Bronte homestead, and knows the country ‘ e
well. |
Mr. J. P. Boland, M.P., lectured on Saturday night, 19th inst., on

Irish trade with foreign countries. Lord MacDonnell presiding, and &

summing up the debate that followed, urged his compatriots 1o sink
capital in native woollen and cloth industries, which were now on the

up grade. He hoped that in this generation on the deserted sites in
the West of Ireland, where the grass-grown furrows showed the evidence
of previous tillage, new villages would spring up, peopled by a happy
and vigorous mankind. There was no reason why the main industries
should be confined to the north-east corner of Ireland. Belfast posses
sed no greater natural advantages in the way of coal and water than any
other part of the island. The decadence which had begun in the south
should be arrested. Ireland was tired of political strife, but ag1tatlon- A
which resulted in material improvement should not he deprecated. The f:fj'
necessity for political agitation would soon cease to exist; a good tlﬂl“;_
was coming soon for Ireland, when all her sons of no matter what creed

or shade of opinion would work together for her regenemtlon and I“'“s al

perity.
